question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Learning Disability Service Framework.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has made of the impact on Northern Ireland manufacturers of their exclusion from the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme, announced by the UK Government on 16 April 2026, given that the scheme applies to Great Britain only.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has sought clarity from HM Treasury on whether any Barnett consequentials will flow to the Northern Ireland Executive from the Exchequer funding element of the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland businesses benefit from the UK Government's Modern Industrial Strategy, given that a flagship intervention of that strategy, the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme, explicitly excludes Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the National Association of Head Teachers' trade dispute over workload.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any assessment that has been made of the pressures currently facing the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the expected timetable for the introduction of the Offshore Renewable Energy Installations Bill.
answer_text: My Department is aiming to introduce the Offshore Renewable Energy Installations Bill in the 2026/27 legislative programme, subject to the formal confirmation of that programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on ensuring special schools have adequate nursing provision to meet the medical needs of pupils.
answer_text: I am committed to working with colleagues in Health and Education to ensure that all children with complex medical needs can be supported to access school and the curriculum through child specific care. Officials from my Department and Department of Health, along with representatives from the Education Authority and the Public Health Agency (PHA), have commenced work to develop a new model of care in special schools that best meets the healthcare needs of these children. This project is informed by the recommendations made in the PHA review of population health of children with complex medical needs attending special schools and it is intended that this work will determine the level of multi-agency resourcing required across professions, including nursing. Engagement with children, parents and families and staff working in special schools including teachers and classroom assistants and staff from the Health Trusts will be a key element of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the support that can be provided to households impacted by the rising cost of oil and fuel.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the impact of the rise in heating oil prices here following the Israeli-led attacks on Iran. This is particularly effecting lower income and vulnerable households. Approximately 60% of our homes, more than half a million homes, use heating oil as their primary source of heating.  In recent weeks I have met heating oil, natural gas, National Energy Action, and wider business organisations to understand first-hand what cost pressures are in the market and the impact these are already having on lower income and vulnerable households and businesses. I have pressed Minister Shanks and Minister McCluskey for urgent support for consumers, and I continue to do so, as the £17million confirmed on Monday 16 March is clearly inadequate to help those who are struggling with rising energy prices.  My officials have engaged with the Competition and Markets Authority, and I welcome its review into the conduct of oil suppliers, intermediaries and brokers, to ensure pricing reflects genuine cost pressures and that behaviour is consistent with consumer protection law. Trading Standards plays a key role in protecting consumers from illegal trading practices, including unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It also provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and advice to consumers on raising complaints.  The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing weekly average prices. Consumers are encouraged to use its website to compare suppliers and find information on oil buying clubs and Fuel Stamp schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department has taken to deliver the £2 billion renewable energy economy target, set out in the Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero Energy, published in December 2021,
answer_text: The Energy Strategy: The Path to Net Zero Energy is an Executive-wide strategy. Delivery of the £2 billion low carbon and renewable energy economy target depends on coordinated action across government, regulators, local authorities, industry and the wider public sector.Within this collective framework, my Department has taken a leading role in creating the conditions for growth in the low-carbon economy. For example, my Department has directly supported economic growth through measures such as the £40 million Energy Resource and Efficiency Programme for business, the Industrial Decarbonisation for Northern Ireland initiative, and the publication of the Green Skills Action Plan to ensure the workforce is equipped for emerging opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the UK–EU Sanitary and Phytosanitary Agreement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when her Department intends to complete the drafting of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The Employment Rights Bill is being drafted by the Office of the Legislative Counsel. It will be completed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rational for not progressing the establishment of a talent development agency, modelled on SkillNet Ireland.
answer_text: I refer you to my recent correspondence AQW 38054 22-27 in which I detailed my Department’s work into the establishment of a talent development agency. I would like to highlight again, that given the limited time remaining in this mandate, significant structural change of this nature will inevitably fall to an incoming Minister. However, the preparatory work we are progressing now will ensure that the next Executive has a clear, evidence-based foundation from which to consider future reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that his Department has spent almost £2.5 billion on IT systems over the last three years, to outline the steps being taken to ensure those systems are kept up to date and fully utilised.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given that reducing health waiting lists was a priority in the Programme for Government 2024-2027, to outline the steps the Delivery Unit has taken to drive progress in this area.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any benefits that sectoral plans can deliver in driving economic growth in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s 3-year Sectoral Action Plans signal clarity of ambition and a pathway to build upon our commercial, innovative and research strengths – through a suite of interventions spanning my department that will drive technological adoption, business investment, collaboration and internationalisation. I will be publishing a mid-point update on the sectoral plans soon, which will highlight delivery and impact to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the reasons why the Northern Ireland economy is growing at more than double the rate of the UK average; and (ii) to detail any steps she is taking to sustain and build upon this performance.
answer_text: Recent data present a broadly encouraging picture for the North’s economy, with our economic output increasing by 2.9% over the past year, which is higher than in Britain. Over the past two quarters, services have been the biggest contributor to our overall economic growth. Performance in production is also encouraging, with all four main sectors expanding over the year.  Construction shows signs of stabilisation, with two consecutive quarters of growth and strong output in infrastructure.In a very challenging economic context, the stronger relative performance of the North’s economy demonstrates the resilience of our businesses and workers. This performance is illustrated in the following table:NICEI / GDPNIUKAnnual Growth2.9%1.3%Quarterly Growth1.0%0.1%Post-Pandemic Growth12.0%5.2%Index of ServicesNIUKOverall Annual Growth4.2%1.6%Business Services and Finance Sector (Annual Growth)7.4%0.9%Index of ProductionNIUKOverall Annual Growth3.8%0.9%Manufacturing Sector (Annual Growth)3.5%1.0%Since my predecessor, Conor Murphy, announced a new Economic Vision for the North in February 2024, significant progress has been made against the four key priorities of: Good Jobs, Productivity, Regional Balance, and Decarbonisation. I intend to sustain and build on the North’s positive economic performance through a continued sharp focus on the delivery of these priorities for the remainder of this mandate.Areas such as the once in a generation reform to employment rights through the Employment Rights Bill, the investments made in addressing priority skills gaps through the Skills Action Plan and Skills Fund, expansion of the university provision in Magee, and the implementation of our new local economic partnerships will all support continued growth. More information on the steps we are taking can be found in my Department’s published 2025-26 Business Plan DfE Business Plan 2025-26 | Department for the Economy and Year Two Progress Report Two Year Progress Report January 2026 - Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the planned open book exercise with Treasury.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the work that is ongoing to raise awareness of the final seven months of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: Since the Scheme opened in August 2021, more than 12,000 applications have been submitted with almost £124 million paid to eligible applicants, offering meaningful recognition of the harm they have suffered.   The Victims’ Payments Board have distributed Information kits to a range of stakeholders, political representatives, and welfare organisations across the UK and further afield, to advertise the Scheme and encourage applications from any victims that may be eligible to apply ahead of the closing date for applications on 30 August 2026. A number of press releases have been circulated recently to media outlets across the UK and Ireland, with more to follow. With the Scheme now in its final year, we would encourage people to apply. Details of groups providing support for applications are available on the Victims Payments Board website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to make any changes to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to ensure that eligible families are not excluded due to (i) provider-level administrative; or (ii) technical, barriers.
answer_text: Participation in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is voluntary and at the discretion of individual childcare providers and schools. While the Department cannot mandate registration, it strongly encourages uptake by offering financial assistance to support administration costs to register for the scheme.   A one-off registration payment is made to providers alongside the initial claim to support set-up on the NICSS portal for processing claims and maintaining records.  This is further supplemented by a monthly administration fee based on the number of children within the provider using the scheme each month.In addition, ongoing practical support is made available through the Early Years Organisation (EYO), which administers the NICSS on behalf of my Department.  EYO provides training and ongoing assistance to help providers and schools implement the scheme smoothly and effectively.   My officials continue to monitor NICSS uptake amongst all types of providers and schools, and EYO will liaise directly with settings on a one to one basis where any issues are identified.  Direct engagement with schools is currently underway through a survey issued on 19 January to understand any barriers or practical challenges schools face in registering for the NICSS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any legal implications the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme legal judgment and subsequent appeal, may have for the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project, given the latter's focus on road safety improvements on an existing strategic route.
answer_text: In light of the A5 Judgement I am taking time to consider the impact on all major infrastructure projects.We have to await the Court of Appeal's ruling, because it is important that we consider it fully when looking at the next steps for all major roads projects, as we do not want to be in a scenario where we face more legal challenges. 	However, my officials are still undertaking necessary work and keeping these schemes live as they are priorities for me.Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community and unfortunately the A1 is a road that has been impacted exponentially by tragic circumstances.  I remain committed to the construction of this vitally important infrastructure safety upgrade and will take all actions available to me to secure its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of whether the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project is exposed to the legal or environmental vulnerabilities, identified in the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme case; and (ii) to detail any factors that distinguish the A1 project from the A5 scheme, in legal and environmental terms.
answer_text: In light of the A5 Judgement I am taking time to consider the impact on all major infrastructure projects.We have to await the Court of Appeal's ruling, because it is important that we consider it fully when looking at the next steps for all major roads projects, as we do not want to be in a scenario where we face more legal challenges. 	However, my officials are still undertaking necessary work and keeping these schemes live as they are priorities for me.Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community and unfortunately the A1 is a road that has been impacted exponentially by tragic circumstances.  I remain committed to the construction of this vitally important infrastructure safety upgrade and will take all actions available to me to secure its delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has carried out any overall evaluation of the Central Good Relations Fund, beyond monitoring individual funded projects.
answer_text: The Central Good Relations Fund has been independently reviewed several times, most recently in 2025. This evaluation is currently being finalised however emerging findings indicate the CRGF is directly supporting progress towards the aims of the T:BUC Strategy, helping organisations to deliver positive Good Relations benefits to participants and their wider communities.The programme was also considered as part of the T:BUC Review and will continue to be a key aspect of delivery as we work towards developing a refreshed approach to Good Relations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any assessment he has made of the impact that the uncertainty around Sport NI funding is having on workforce retention.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment his Department has made of the potential impact of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, which has been introduced in the Republic of Ireland, on the Single Electricity Market.
answer_text: The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) came into effect on 1 January 2026.In regard to any potential impact on the trade in electricity, in respect of movement from GB into NI, there will be no CBAM liability, given that the EU CBAM Regulation does not fall within scope of the Windsor Framework. There will also be no CBAM liability on electricity imported into RoI from NI, as both jurisdictions are subject to the same carbon price under the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) (electricity generating installations in NI continue to participate in the EU ETS under the terms of the Windsor Framework, to protect the operation of the all Ireland Single Electricity Market).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rational as to why renewable energy, imported from Scotland or the Republic of Ireland, does not count towards Northern Ireland’s renewable energy targets.
answer_text: Reporting of electricity consumption and renewable generation statistics began in 2014 and has not included renewable electricity generation from either the Republic of Ireland or Scotland. In reporting to date, renewable electricity generation refers to local metered renewable generation.		The Energy Strategy committed to reviewing how this measure is calculated. A user engagement exercise took place from 4 September to 31 October 2025 (statistical user engagement paper) with the findings published on 8 January 2026 in the statistical user engagement report. These documents explain the new methodology including why renewable electricity generation from Scotland or the Republic of Ireland is not included. 			The first statistical report based on the new methodology will be published in March 2026 and officials can provide further clarifications after this if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether Northern Ireland receives the statistical benefits of counting renewable energy, imported from the Republic of Ireland and Scotland, into the local grid.
answer_text: The reporting of electricity consumption and renewable generation statistics from 2014 to date has not included renewable electricity generation from either the Republic of Ireland or Scotland. In reporting to date, renewable electricity generation refers to local metered renewable generation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will follow the UK Government’s decision to reduce green levies on household energy bills, by making similar changes in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Autumn Budget included measures which will reduce domestic electricity bills in Britain by £150 annually for a three-year period beginning in April 2026. 	This is made up of, firstly, removing the Energy Company Obligation. The Energy Company Obligation never applied here, thus is not part of electricity consumers’ bills. 	In addition to this, Treasury will fund, from April, 75% of the domestic share of the Renewables Obligation. The NI Renewables Obligation is part of domestic electricity bills here and Finance officials are engaging with Treasury to agree how a similar level of support would feed through to domestic electricity bills here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions his Department has had with the UK Government, regarding the potential alignment of UK policy with the EU on the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, since its introduction in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) was introduced on 1 January 2026. As the Regulation has not been added to the scope of the Windsor Framework, it does not extend any obligation to NI based importers, including with respect to imports from GB.On 26 November 2025, the UK Government announced its intention to legislate in the 2025-26 Finance Bill to introduce a UK CBAM from 1 January 2027, and they have confirmed that it will apply across the whole of the UK, including Northern Ireland. They have stated their intention to continue to work with international partners, including the EU, to ensure implementation in a way that works for businesses.Both I and my officials liaise closely with, and receive regular updates from, our counterparts across the relevant UK Government Departments with respect to CBAM policy. Through this engagement both I and my officials have stressed the importance of considering the interaction with the EU CBAM.In May 2025, the UK Government and European Union agreed, as part of the Summit on their future partnership, to work towards linking the UK and EU Emissions Trading Schemes.  Negotiations are currently underway between the parties. The establishment of a link between these two schemes would create the conditions for mutual CBAM exemptions, and I have written to counterparts in the UK Government to highlight the importance of this work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she expects the first auction under the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme, to take place.
answer_text: Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance.My officials are prioritising the Primary Bill needed to make the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme operational. Once the primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.These powers will enable us to run auctions at the right time, securing the best technologies and capacity to meet our targets at a fair price for consumers. The timeline for the first auction will be confirmed alongside the consultation on the Terms and Conditions, which is expected later in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any plans Invest NI has to acquire additional economic land, in the Lagan Valley constituency.
answer_text: Through its Regional Property Strategy, Invest NI has outlined its approach to addressing gaps in the supply of industrial and economic land across the North. This includes working closely with local councils, enterprise agencies, and other partners to co-design property solutions to address the needs of individual council areas.Invest NI does not disclose specific locations or sites it may consider for future land acquisitions. This approach helps ensure best value for the public purse and reduces the risk of speculative activity that could impact the cost or viability of potential economic development projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of Invest NI's economic land, currently available within the Lagan Valley constituency.
answer_text: Invest NI holds 220 acres of land within the Lagan Valley constituency, of which c1.7 acres remain available for Invest NI customers with an approved business case and an immediate demonstrable land need.  Through its Regional Property Programme, Invest NI has committed to work in partnership with local stakeholders and councils, to identify property requirements and collectively deliver solutions that support long-term economic development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) work undertaken; and (ii) progress made by her Department, since September 2025, towards the development of a skills support service in Northern Ireland, comparable to Skillnet Ireland.
answer_text: I welcomed the constructive debate in the Assembly in September on addressing skills gaps between young people and businesses, including the proposal to explore a talentdevelopment agency model.Since then, my officials have been exploring how best to strengthen employer-led skills development in the North, including examining models such as Skillnet Ireland.  This issue also featured in my recent engagements with the NI Chamber, who have identified the establishment of a Workforce Development Agency as a key policy ask. I have asked my officials to engage directly with NI Chamber to scope this proposal in more detail and assess how such a model could support our wider skills strategy. Their insights will inform ongoing departmental consideration of how best to enhance the skills support landscape.While any significant structural reform is likely to be a matter for a future Executive, preparatory work is underway to ensure a clear evidence base, clear options, and appropriate engagement mechanisms are in place to support informed decisionmaking in the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5726/22-27, to detail a timescale for the completion of the A1 Junctions Phase 2, between Hillsborough and Loughbrickland.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project will deliver enhanced safety measures along the A1 route, costing in the region of £120 - £130m and will include the closing up of all gaps in the central median and the provision of a number of new grade separated junctions.The scheme was confirmed by my predecessor as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 and a restricted list was notified to tenderers on 9 May 2025. Since the judgment on the A5 Western Transport Corridor project and the Department’s appeal, I am taking time to carefully consider the potential impact on other projects as they move through the next stages of their delivery, this includes the A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project.While we await the ruling from the Court Appeal, my officials continue to work on the next steps in relation to the project and I remain committed to the delivery of this vitally important safety scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any roads-related issues that have been raised with her Department, as a result of PSNI investigations into road traffic collisions at the Soldierstown Road A26 junction , including any consideration that has been given to each issue.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.We are not aware of any specific PSNI investigations that have been undertaken at this location, however the Department receives information on personal injury road traffic collisions on an annual basis from the PSNI which is used to help prioritise these interventions to ensure maximum benefit from the limited resources available.Data from the last three years confirms that there have been no recorded personal injury collisions at this junction and it would therefore not be considered for any remedial works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any progress made in delivering the multi-year funding programme for sport, that was indicated during the consultation on Sport NI funding arrangements.
answer_text: The consultation on multiyear Sport NI funding relates to the disbursement of National Lottery funding, and is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised as follows:“During the consultation on Sport NI funding arrangements, stakeholders expressed strong support for a multi-year funding approach to provide greater stability and enable longer-term planning for sports organisations.“Sport NI has been progressing work on the design of a multi-annual funding programme, which will align with strategic priorities and ensure robust governance and accountability.“The programme aims to deliver improved certainty for funded bodies, reduce administrative burden, and support sustainable development of sport across Northern Ireland.“Sport NI are currently working towards the development and implementation of a multi-annual funding programme for sport. Timelines and detailed arrangements are currently being finalised, and further information will be communicated publicly once this work is complete. Information on this programme will be released in due course.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has approved a funding programme for Sport NI for the 2026–2027 financial year.
answer_text: As my Department has not yet been allocated a budget for 2026-27, I have not yet approved a funding programme for Sport NI for the 2026-27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether (i) he has met, in the last six months; or (ii) plans to meet, representatives from the sporting sector to address concerns regarding funding uncertainty and governance.
answer_text: I meet representatives from the sporting sector on a regular basis, and will continue to do so. A list of my meetings are published on a quarterly basis on the OpenData NI website.Funding and governance issues are frequently raised at these meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rational as to why a multi-year funding programme for Sport NI has not yet been implemented, despite commitments made to the sporting sector during consultation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Following Sport NI’s engagement with Sport over the summer and the feedback gathered through consultation in the autumn, Sport NI continue to work through the development process for the new programme.“Aligned to the Programme Outcomes published in the consultation document, this new programme will focus on;“Growth in participation levels, particularly among those who are inactive or face the greatest barriers. “Increased access to sport & physical activity for people from disadvantaged areas and those living in poverty. “Broader representation in sport, with higher participation from under-represented groups. “Improved retention through better quality and more engaging participant experiences. “Increased numbers of NI athletes transitioning on to senior National Programmes (GBR/IRL/NIR). “Increased diversity across performance sport (athlete demographics and workforce). “Sport NI remains committed to introducing a multi-annual funding programme for sport and is working to develop a more focused allocation model based on these policy objectives. Sport NI will give Sports as much information as possible and as early as possible to enable longer-term planning. Future levels of investment may vary from previous levels of investment, and Sport NI will work with each Governing Body to manage their specific circumstances.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the total cost of the Knockmore railway line feasibility study.
answer_text: The “Shaping the Future” document that was published in December 2025, shows the capital cost to reopen the line will vary depending on the final service pattern selected, the airport connection and whether the line is re-opened with overhead electrification or using alternative traction. The estimate to undertake the on-site surveys, design work, planning and development to reach the next major milestone, the Outline Business Case, is £10-£15m. The total cost estimate ranges from £300m - £500m, however, the costs for a project will only be finalised after completion of the Outline Business Case.I cannot provide detailed breakdown of the total cost of the feasibility study, as the material forms part of an ongoing policy development process and includes potentially commercially sensitive details. Releasing it now could lead to misunderstanding and compromise the integrity of the required decision-making and procurement for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will publish a redacted version of the Knockmore railway line feasibility study.
answer_text: My Department has considered the option of releasing a redacted version of the feasibility study. However, even with redactions, large sections of the document contain material that is integral to ongoing policy development and commercially sensitive information. Removing these sections would result in a document that is incomplete and potentially misleading, which could lead to misunderstanding among stakeholders and the public.Furthermore, publishing any version at this stage could compromise the integrity of future decision-making and procurement processes. For these reasons, the Department believes that withholding the study in its entirety until these risks have passed is the most appropriate course of action.It is important to note that the major findings and strategic considerations from the feasibility study are already reflected in the published report Shaping the Future – A Rail Network for Northern Ireland, which provides a comprehensive overview of the Department’s vision for rail development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on whether any acceptable geotechnical submissions have been received by her Department from the developer's consultants, in relation to the Ayrshire development, Lisburn.
answer_text: I have been advised that the Ayrshire Development in Lisburn has applied for Geotechnical Certification under CD622 Managing Geotechnical Risk of the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges under the title Brokerstown Road.The developer is currently progressing the various stages required of CD622 and has completed the Statement of Intent and a combined Preliminary Sources Study and Geotechnical Investigation Report stage. I advised in October 2025, certification of the combined report stage has not been released as the correct geotechnical certificate has not yet been received by my Department and this remains the position. The Geotechnical Advisor reviewed and commented on the Geotechnical Design Report in April 2025 which is the final phase before construction can commence. Subject to the developer satisfactorily addressing the comments made as a result of this review, geotechnical certification may be deemed acceptable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department’s rational for not publishing the full feasibility study into the Knockmore railway line.
answer_text: I have decided that the feasibility study cannot be released at this stage as the material forms part of an ongoing policy development process and includes potentially commercially sensitive details. Releasing it now could lead to misunderstanding and compromise the integrity of the required decision-making and procurement for the project.  I can advise you that the feasibility study has contributed into an initial Strategic Outline Case for the project. I have previously allocated funding, this financial year, that has enabled priority tasks to progress, including more detailed technical work on the proposed connection to Belfast International Airport, engagement with planning authorities, signalling and electrification development and assessment of the user worked crossings along the route.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a breakdown of the estimated cost identified, to progress the Knockmore railway line to outline business case stage.
answer_text: The current assessment of funding required to progress the Antrim to Knockmore project to OBC stage is £10-15 million.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any decisions that remain outstanding within the Executive to progress the regeneration of the Maze Long Kesh site.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget 2025 announcement on reducing energy bills across the UK, to detail (i) when this measure will take effect in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether she is aware of any obstacles that could delay its implementation, compared with the rest of the UK.
answer_text: The autumn budget outlined measures to reduce household energy bills by funding part of the costs associated with the Renewables Obligation (RO) and by deciding not to renew the Energy Company Obligation (ECO), both of which apply only in Britain.		 Officials are seeking clarity from British government colleagues on comparability of schemes in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether provisions will be put in place to ensure the Just Transition Commission will act in the interests of sectors such as agriculture and fisheries as we decarbonise our economy.
answer_text: I am currently seeking agreement from my Executive colleagues for regulations to establish the Just Transition Commission. Those regulations require the Commission to include representatives from those sectors mandated by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and also additional sectors that were identified through the consultation and stakeholder engagement process to ensure that the interests of all emissions sectors, including agriculture and fisheries, are represented.The Commission, once established, will have a key advisory and oversight role in ensuring that all departments, in developing and progressing decarbonisation plans, take account of the Just Transition Principle and the 11 objectives set out in the Act.  Within the 11 objectives, to ensure that the interests of sectors are at the forefront, is a requirement to support sectors of the economy in Northern Ireland that are likely to be most affected by action to reduce emissions as well as creating decent, fair and high-value work in a way which does not negatively affect the current workforce.To support the work of the Commission, I have proposed a provision in the draft regulations that it will have the ability to establish Working Groups or Committees, which will allow it to call on wider sector views if required, to support it in the delivery of its advisory and oversight role.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a biomethane policy.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress its biomethane actions as part of the 2025 Energy Strategy action plan including progressing support mechanisms, consulting on options for gas network connection costs, and investigating standards/certifications.		Work is ongoing to analyse a range of options aimed at addressing the costs for biomethane producers connecting to the gas network. Additionally, following a Call for Evidence on gas quality standards, the Department is reviewing responses to identify a preferred policy approach to the level of permitted oxygen content for gas entering the distribution or transmission networks. A joint public consultation on the treatment of connection costs and gas quality standards is planned for early 2026.		Alongside this, my Department will continue to collaborate with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), the Utility Regulator, and other key stakeholders to support the development of a sustainable biomethane sector.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any consideration given to the allocation of funds from the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland to the (i) redevelopment of Casement Park; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: I refer the Member to AQW 34686/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10479/22-27 and AQW 18185/22-27, whether (i) the vesting process for the land required for the construction of the B101 North Lisburn Feeder Road at Thaxton, Lisburn, has been completed, given that the original estimated timeframe of up to one year has now elapsed; and (ii) her Department has received any indication from the developers as to when construction is expected to commence.
answer_text: My officials are exploring options to purchase the land by agreement, therefore the vesting process has not yet commenced.Construction of the road can only begin once the land has been purchased or vested, with this process likely to take up to a year to complete.  As such, I cannot confirm a date for the commencement of construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why there has been a further delay in the publishing of the feasibility study for the reopening of the Knockmore Line; and (ii) when she expects the results of the study to be published.
answer_text: As I have previously said, the report examining the reopening of the Knockmore Railway Line, which would reconnect Antrim and Lisburn via Crumlin, Glenavy, and Ballinderry, and a link to Belfast International Airport, has recently been submitted to my department by Translink. Once I have had time to consider the report’s findings, I plan to publish its main outcomes in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rational for having no provision for a therapy dog, in the Lisburn Local IMPACT Team, as part of the Special Educational Needs Transformation Programme.
answer_text: The SEND Local IMPACT Team model was developed on a budget neutral basis and therefore, as this provision was not previously offered through the individual Education Authority SEN Services it was not incorporated in to the model that went live in September 2025 including the Lisburn Local IMPACT Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department’s projected capital budget needs.
answer_text: AQO 2663 22-27 - ANSWER Our justice system is currently delivered from facilities that are often antiquated and use outdated technology. A substantial increase in future capital investment is vital if we are to upgrade and modernise the ageing justice estate.  The planned capital projects for the four years, 2026-27 to 2029-30, would cost on average of £270 million each year. This would require significant additional funding above the current £100 million Capital budget for my Department.  These capital bids reflect the costs of a number of key Departmental projects for police, courts, prisons and forensic science, many of which are included within the draft Investment Strategy.  There is an overarching need to further invest in ambitious transformation programmes across the Justice estate and to support and sustain investments in estates, with a focus on rehabilitation and clear invest to save opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement that has taken place between Tourism NI, Invest NI, and Cricket Ireland, to explore Northern Ireland’s involvement in the International Cricket Council Men’s T20 World Cup 2030.
answer_text: There has been engagement between officials in Tourism NI and Cricket Ireland to discuss the current status and opportunities that hosting International Cricket Council Men’s T20 World Cup 2030 games in the north may bring. 		Tourism NI, along with officials from the Department for Communities, had an initial meeting with Cricket Ireland in September 2024. This was followed up with another meeting in October 2025 between Tourism NI and PR representatives acting on behalf of Cricket Ireland. Further to this engagement, Tourism NI is arranging a follow up meeting in Belfast with Cricket Ireland representatives, Tourism Ireland, Invest NI, and Tourism NI to explore the feasibility of hosting parts of the tournament in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether an assessment has been made of the potential economic and tourism benefits for Northern Ireland, in hosting matches during the International Cricket Council Men’s T20 World Cup 2030.
answer_text: Tourism NI officials have met with Cricket Ireland to discuss the event. On the back of these discussions exploratory work is underway to consider the feasibility of hosting matches in the North.Consideration of the wider benefits the event may bring including PR and broadcast opportunities, as well as economic impact, is underway but detailed information is not yet available to undertake a full assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether an assessment has been made of how hosting part of the International Cricket Council Men’s T20 World Cup in 2030, could contribute to community participation, inclusion, and wellbeing.
answer_text: I instructed my officials to engage with Cricket Ireland and the Northern Ireland Civil Service Sports Association regarding their plans to upgrade the cricket ground at Stormont.  It is evident that the proposals support the Department’s policy objectives of promoting sport, physical activity and community wellbeing. As with all major projects, the funding request remains subject to the normal business case approval process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why her Department's recently updated private streets enforcement guidance does not apply to long-standing developments where road adoption remains outstanding.
answer_text: The new guidance establishes a threshold for the initiation of enforcement action of 2 years following 80% occupation of houses in a development.  It also amends the bond release thresholds to ensure the developer completes more work before any of the bond can be released.  These changes significantly reduce the risk of there being insufficient funds available in the bond to complete the works should enforcement be required.The long-term backlog of developments primarily consists of sites where the cost of works to bring the development to an adoptable standard exceeds the funds held in the bond and the developer is no longer trading.Unfortunately, for these longer term unadopted developments, the funding from the bond that would allow us to commence enforcement under the new guidance is simply not available and my Department is not funded to bridge any shortfall.  Furthermore, there is no legislative requirement for my Department to do so.My Department has taken significant steps to reduce the risk of new developments entering the unadopted backlog through the introduction of the updated guidance and increased the bond calculation rates.  However, unless an alternative funding source is identified for older sites which bridges the funding gap between the bond value and the cost of works to adopt a site, my department cannot compete the works and adopt the development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) purpose; (ii) necessary provisions; (iii) financial implications; and (iv) timescale, for the First Stage introduction of the Onshore Petroleum Licensing Bill.
answer_text: The Petroleum Exploration and Licensing (Repeal) Bill will introduce a ban on all forms of onshore petroleum exploration and production in the North through amendments to The Petroleum (Production) Act (Northern Ireland) 1964 and related regulations. There are no financial implications associated with the Bill’s introduction. The Bill is in the final drafting stage, and I plan to introduce it to the Assembly in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what issues have occurred with the linkage of encompass to online data systems, in the current financial year.
answer_text: There have been no significant issues with the linkages between encompass and online data systems in the current financial year.  The encompass solution links to selected Regional and Trust systems to augment patient care information stored within the system and support the patient journey. These links are complex and the encompass programme works closely with all vendors to ensure they are monitored and maintained, with any issues quickly identified, triaged and resolved by encompass' 24/7 managed support service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the purpose of IT projects such as encompass is to deliver reduced administration, to outline why it has not freed up budget for front-line service delivery in health and social care.
answer_text: Digital transformation projects such as encompass are strategic investments. While encompass is now implemented across Northern Ireland, a number of Trusts are still in the post-go-live phase, focusing on embedding the system and ensuring operational stability. Given the scale of transformation, it is expected that cost savings and service improvements emerge as the changes are embedded.Positive outcomes for staff, patients and services users already being seen through encompass and other key digital systems include improved access to information, enhanced clinical decision making and streamlined workflows. Work is continuing to identify, baseline and realise benefits across projects and the wider HSCNI service.All HSC IT enabled projects and programmes are committed to ongoing monitoring and evaluation to ensure they deliver value for money and support reinvestment into front-line services across HSCNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the (i) purpose; (ii) necessary provisions; (iii) financial implications; and (iv) timescale, for the introduction of offshore renewable energy installation legislation.
answer_text: The purpose of offshore renewable energy installation legislation is to establish frameworks for decommissioning, safety zones and rights of navigation within our territorial waters.The necessary provisions and financial implications will be detailed in a primary bill, which has been temporarily deferred until 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what overrun costs have occurred on his Department’s IT projects since the 2022-23 financial year; and (ii) what investigations there have been into these overruns.
answer_text: Since the 2022–2023 financial year, cost overruns have been incurred in two of my Department’s major IT programmes due to changes in approval processes; the NIDIS programme, which saw an uplift of £3.461 million and the Encompass programme, which experienced an increase of £24.9 million.  Approval changes were investigated and managed through a standard business case addendum process to ensure continued alignment with the NICS Five Case Model. NIDISThe Full Business Case for NIDIS was approved in November 2020 with an estimated cost of £12.235 million. A revised approval was issued in November 2024 to reflect timeline adjustments, increasing the estimated cost to £15.716 million, an uplift of £3.461 million.For NIDIS, the approval uplift was managed through a formal revision process, endorsed by both the Department of Health and the Department of Finance, ensuring continued compliance with the +10% tolerance threshold. The encompass ProgrammeThe full business base for encompass was approved in July 2020 with an estimated cost of £330.9 million. An addendum to the business case was subsequently approved in December 2023, revising the cost to £355.8 million, an increase of £24.9 million.While the original case for encompass remained unchanged, a business case addendum was submitted to the Department in 2022 and subsequently approved. This was prompted by recruitment challenges within the regional encompass team, the impacts of COVID-19, greater clarity around the underlying digital infrastructure, and the need for enhanced clinical and professional support at Trust level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of domestic abuse on women and children in rural communities.
answer_text: AQO 2514 22-27 - ANSWER I recognise that domestic abuse has a profound impact on women and children in all parts of our society, but there are particular challenges in rural communities.  Along with Minister Muir I recently visited Women’s Aid in Armagh and Down to mark Rural Crime Week where we heard about the barriers for those in rural areas accessing services and support.  Geographic isolation, limited access to services, lack of public transport, financial dependency and stigma can all compound risk and make it harder for victims to seek help and in some cases, perpetrators exploit these barriers to maintain control.  Through the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy my Department is working with partners to understand better the needs of victims of domestic abuse living in rural areas and to improve provision to meet those needs. My Department funds a number of support services for victims of crime and for victims of domestic abuse, including ASSIST NI which operates across the whole of Northern Ireland, including rural areas, to provide advocacy services to high-risk victims of domestic abuse, to support them through the criminal justice process and help them to access other relevant services. There are other initiatives that my Department is leading, including the establishment of Remote Evidence Centres, that are helping to reduce under-reporting and attrition, by increasing confidence in the Justice system and providing venues where victims can feel safe as they provide evidence to the court. In addition, through the DSA Small Grant Scheme, funding is being provided to voluntary and community sector organisations that deliver specialist support on the ground, including in rural communities, ensuring that services are accessible regardless of geography. The 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline, jointly funded by my Department, also provides immediate, confidential support to anyone affected, whether they live in an urban or rural setting, and can signpost to local services. Domestic abuse is a devastating crime with long-lasting impacts, and I remain committed to working in partnership to ensure that victims in rural communities have the same level of access to protection and support as those in more urban areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of the pension tax implications for consultants caused by delays in implementing pay awards as recommended by the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration; and (ii)  to outline what steps will be taken to ensure timely implementation in future years.
answer_text: I am aware of the pension tax implications for consultants caused by delays in implementing pay awards. In terms of the rules about how pension input amounts are treated for tax purposes, HMT/HMRC sets these rules. As tax is a reserved matter, these rules apply consistently across the UK. All pension schemes must apply these rules.With regard to implementation of pay awards in future years, I am committed towards implementing the pay awards in a timely manner. My aim has been, and will be in the years to come, to have the pay award in Northern Ireland paid to a similar timescale as in England. My officials are actively working with the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration to ensure its recommendations are received as early as possible, allowing sufficient time to secure and implement the pay award. However, there is a need to recognise the significant budgetary pressures and the difficult financial circumstances currently facing my Department.Additionally, as with any pay award, there is a process that must be followed before it is paid to staff. My Department require approval from the Department of Finance prior to issuing a revised pay circular. From the date of issue of the circular, BSO require a period of 12 weeks to request, test and implement the required IT changes.I recognise the importance of avoiding delays in implementing pay awards and I remain committed to ensuring that, for now and in the future, our HSC workforce is fairly rewarded for their vital contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when funding will be made available to implement recommendations from the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration Fifty Third Report: 2025, given the pension tax consequences caused by delays in recent years.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of each Minister to manage all pressures including pay costs within their department’s allocated budget. I am committed to working collectively with all my Executive colleagues to resolve the current issues.We cannot deliver public services without public servants, and it is essential that all public sector workers, including health and social care staff, are appropriately rewarded and receive any pay award in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the urgent steps her Department is taking to establish, a co-design group to redesign the Go Succeed enterprise support model, based on the findings of the interim evaluation.
answer_text: My Department has no role in the administration of Go Succeed, which is funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. It was expected that this funding would be transferred to the Executive following the British Government's Spending Review announcement in June.	In anticipation of that announcement my Department was ready to establish a co-design group. However, the British Government has instead created a role for the NIO. I have written to the Secretary of State to ask for clarity on the future of this vital programme. Disappointingly that clarity has not yet been provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the urgent steps her Department is taking to (i) secure funding certainty for the future of the Go Succeed programme; and (ii) ensure a redesigned model can be implemented from April 2026.
answer_text: The Department of Finance leads on negotiations concerning the Local Growth Fund. However, with the permission of the Finance Minister, I have written to the Secretary of State to ask for clarity in relation to Go Succeed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to secure specialist nurses in an effort to reduce waiting lists.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the weight limit proposal for heavy goods vehicles in Royal Hillsborough will be published.
answer_text: An assessment of the suitability of alternative routes following a request for a weight limit restriction to be introduced in the whole village, including Carnreagh, has been commissioned.This work is nearing completion and officials will finalise the report in late November. I would hope to be in a position to provide a further update in December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on whether any acceptable geotechnical submissions have been received by her Department from the developer's consultants, in relation to the Ayrshire development, Lisburn.
answer_text: I have been advised that the Ayrshire Development in Lisburn has applied for Geotechnical Certification under CD622 Managing Geotechnical Risk of the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges under the title Brokerstown Road.The developer is currently progressing the various stages required of CD622 and has completed the Statement of Intent and a combined Preliminary Sources Study and Geotechnical Investigation Report stage. Certification of the combined report stage has not been released as the correct geotechnical certificate has not yet been received by my Department. The Geotechnical Advisor reviewed and commented on the Geotechnical Design Report in April 2025 which is the final phase before construction can commence. Subject to the developer satisfactorily addressing the comments made as a result of this review, geotechnical certification may be deemed acceptable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when the revised private streets enforcement guidance will be finalised and published.
answer_text: The new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance for DfI officials was implemented on 25 September 2025.  I announced its introduction on the same date.  The Guidance can be viewed on the Departments website .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the options her Department considered to provide a right turn lane from the A26 Glenavy Road on to Station Road, Moira; and (ii) the rationale for why each option was found to be unviable.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department has considered a range of options to provide a right turn lane on the A26 Glenavy Road into Station Road. These include the realignment of Magheramesk Lane and alteration to the Station Road junction to provide right turning pockets into Station Road or to both Magheramesk Lane and Station Road. These different layouts require the purchase of agricultural land and the realignment of public utility equipment.  There are also a   number of substandard design features, which include a substandard alternative route using the Maghaberry Road.Due to concerns regarding road safety the scheme has not been progressed, and costs to complete such a scheme would be significant compared with the benefits that would be realised.My officials will continue to keep this junction layout under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what barriers her Department has identified that prevent young people, not in education, employment or training, from accessing education, employment or training.
answer_text: The reported increase of young people here not in education, employment or training is of significant concern to me. This is a complex issue, involving a diverse range of young people aged between 16 and 24 with multiple factors at play.  I have asked my officials to consider the factors associated with the increase, for example the increasing incidence of poor mental health which could be contributing to this worrying trend. However, identifying and addressing the barriers will require a holistic response from across the Executive, and I am committed to working with my Executive colleagues to ensure that all our young people can fully participate and achieve their potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the delivery of the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of young people with special educational needs who are not in education, employment or training.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) produce Economic and Labour market statistics which includes data in relation to young people who are not in education, employment or training.  The data produced does not include a breakdown based on the Special Educational Needs (SEN) categorisation.  The term SEN is only used and applicable in the school system, where SEN is defined as a learning difficulty which calls for special educational provision to be made. Consequently, the information you have requested is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline why the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme was not published within the second quarter of 2025, as committed to in the Department’s Business Plan of March 2025 and again in evidence to the Committee for the Economy in June 2025.
answer_text: The Final Scheme Design for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) was published on 19 September 2025.Delivering additional renewable electricity generation is complex, demanding a careful balance between security of supply, affordability, and legislative compliance.I am confident that we are building the right foundations to ensure the scheme delivers the best outcomes for energy consumers and that local communities benefit from the transition to net zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when legislation for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme will be introduced in the Assembly, to enable the first auction to take place as planned in the third quarter of 2026.
answer_text: My Department is targeting to introduce the required primary legislation for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) to the Assembly before the end of this year. Once the primary Bill is in place, subordinate legislation will be taken forward to set out the detailed arrangements.	The timetable for the first auction is set out in the Final Scheme Design for the REPG which was published on 19 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when the final design of the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee scheme will be published.
answer_text: The Final Scheme Design for the Renewable Electricity Price Guarantee (REPG) was published on 19 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any discussions he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to further investment in sports facilities.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timeline for the Department’s civil law reform review of shared property management.
answer_text: ANSWERFollowing the conclusion of a detailed scoping report, I am engaging with officials to determine what further progress can be made on the regulation of shared property management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what engagement has taken place with residents and stakeholders as part of the civil law reform review of shared property management.
answer_text: ANSWERAs part of the detailed scoping study undertaken as part of the civil law reform review of shared property management, meetings were held with key stakeholders including MLAs, housing associations, co-ownership groups, residential conveyancers and with colleagues in government departments. Officials also considered correspondence from home-owners and their representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will follow the UK Government in removing the requirement for apprentices, over the age of 19, to achieve level 2 essential skills in English and Maths.
answer_text: Skills and training are devolved matters and each administration has developed its own training and apprenticeship strategies. There are no current plans to remove this requirement for apprentices over the age of 19. Essential Skills in Communication, Application of Number and ICT are an integral part of the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, where apprentices do not hold a GCSE equivalent in the respective subject. This is to help ensure that apprentices are equipped not only with job-specific technical abilities but also with the core competencies needed to progress in their learning and in their career pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how her Department’s current Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland supports the deployment of renewable liquid gases, such as BioLPG, as part of Northern Ireland’s decarbonisation pathway, particularly for off-grid rural homes.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy, Path to Net Zero, sets the long-term direction to reach net zero carbon by 2050, with a key principle of replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy. In keeping with this principle, my Department is progressing work to ensure a holistic approach is taken to heat policy, we have recently consulted on offering support for low carbon heating technologies such as heat pumps and have also published a Call for Evidence for both liquid biofuels and biomethane, as we continue to explore their respective roles in the energy transition. My department continues to work in collaboration across government and with stakeholders to ensure that the transition to net zero is fair, inclusive, and sustainable for all energy users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the renewable liquid gas sector in relation to investment in local production and supply chains.
answer_text: My officials have continued to engage with the sector on consumer issues and developments in liquid biofuels, particularly in the context of the Departments recent Call for Evidence on the use of biofuels to decarbonise heating. The Department is currently working through the responses and undertaking a detailed analysis. Further engagement with stakeholders in the sector is expected as part of this ongoing process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration has been given to conducting an organisational review of Sport NI, to ensure it delivers effectively for grassroots and elite sports.
answer_text: Sport NI have advised that while reviews of the effectiveness of programmes are carried out on an ongoing basis to ensure Sport NI programmes deliver effectively for grassroots and elite sports, there are no immediate plans for any further formal organisational reviews of Sport NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount received by the Executive in Barnett Consequentials arising from uplifts to Research and Development spending in England announced by the UK Government, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The Executive received £14.833 million of net Barnett consequentials arising from changes to Research and Development spending in England in the last five financial years.  This includes increased consequentials of £17.263 million from spending uplifts and reduced consequentials of £2.430 million from spending reductions.The Executive also received Barnett consequentials from previous Spending Reviews. However, as these are based on departmental outcomes and are not broken down by individual measure, we cannot identify the detail of the Barnett consequentials received. Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what consideration she is giving to the redesign of the enterprise support ecosystem, including the potential re-establishment of the entrepreneurship co-design group.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress made on the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant Eligibility Criteria.
answer_text: Members will be aware that my Department recently concluded a twelve-week public consultation exercise on the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants. The consultation closed on 14 February this year and sought views on options including widening access to more low-income families and also options that included the provision of universal free school meals. 661 responses were submitted either via the online questionnaire or as written responses. I would like to thank those that took the time to share their views on this important issue. My officials have completed their analysis of the responses and a summary is being finalised for publication.  I intend to make my decision on the way forward in the near future. This decision will be informed by the consultation feedback and the potential impact on children and their families, schools and school caterers. However, a key aspect that I cannot simply ignore is the issue of affordability.  My Department continues to face significant funding pressures and is struggling to support existing key services. There are potentially significant budgetary implications of increasing the numbers of children eligible for free school meals and I will need to take these implications into account to ensure that any change is sustainable going forward.I fully recognise that both free school meals and uniform grants provide vital help to many low-income families. For some children, access to a nutritious school lunch may be their main meal of the day. Taking a school lunch plays an important role in supporting a child’s health, wellbeing and learning. I am therefore committed to making a decision on the way forward that balances all the relevant factors in ensuring that we can support those who need it the most.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the key learnings from the transformation of Hydebank Wood Secure College and Women’s Prison during the last ten years.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 2071/22-27			ANSWERThe transformation of Hydebank Wood to a secure college shows the importance of having a clear vision; determined leadership and strong partnerships when delivering successful fundamental change on this scale.The vision began with the recommendation made in the Prison Review Team report in 2011.  This provided the drive and authority to do something that was boldly different at Hydebank.The Hydebank College Implementation team, led by the Prison Service, took the recommendation to establish a secure college and turned it into reality.  This team, and successive leadership teams at Hydebank, have provided strategic direction, navigated the complexities of change, fostered a culture of innovation, and inspired staff to embrace the vision for the establishment - Changing Minds and Changing Lives. The success of Hydebank Wood is also a testament to the power of working in partnership.  Hydebank has worked closely with Belfast Metropolitan College to deliver educational provision that is directly relevant to the needs of the people who live there. The College has also been supported by strong collaboration with a wider network of dedicated individuals and organisations which have all contributed to its development.In May, Hydebank Wood celebrated 10 years since it transitioned to being a secure college, that successful model was built upon and continues to operate with these key learning points in mind.  In 2013, Hydebank was criticised by Inspectors, last year it became the first prison in Northern Ireland to achieve the highest possible inspection outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on what sporting facilities stand to benefit from the Shared Island Fund in the next five years.
answer_text: I have not corresponded with the Irish government in relation to the Shared Island Fund.As I stated in my response to AQW 6098/22-27, I continue to look forward to working with the widest range of funding partners where there is an opportunity to improve investment in communities, including in sporting facilities in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the planned Strategic Rating Reviews for 2025-26.
answer_text: The reviews for 2025/26 are for the Small Business Rate Relief and Non Domestic Vacant rating policy are already underway. These will consist of three main elements:-1.	Desk based research on policy including comparative practice in other jurisdictions and taxbase analysis from the new 2025/26 data.2.	Formal engagement with stakeholders.3.	Surveying of business groups on policies.This work will be conducted within my Department to allow policy proposals to be developed and consulted upon later in the year. This would facilitate any agreed legislative changes to be made by April 2026.I met with a roundtable of business stakeholders on 6 May 2025 to outline my next steps for this area of work, and my officials have already met with key stakeholders to explain the format of the reviews.I have met with officials and the formal Terms of Reference is in the process of being finalised and will be published in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5724/22-27, for an update on the Knockmore Line feasibility study.
answer_text: I can confirm that Translink received funding, through the British Department of Transport’s Union Connectivity Fund, to undertake a feasibility study regarding the re-opening of the railway line between Lisburn and Antrim. As well as considering engineering requirements, demand forecasting and cost estimates, the study is considering reopening the three stations previously in operation on this line at Ballinderry, Glenavy and Crumlin. In addition, three locations for a new station at Belfast International Airport are also being appraised to identify the preferred option. The report is due to be completed in Summer 2025 and I look forward to reviewing its recommendations on how to improve connectivity across our island.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his Department's assessment of repurposing the former Dromore Central Primary School site.
answer_text: The Education Authority is holding the former Dromore Primary School site from disposal as it is currently under consideration for Special Educational Need (SEN) provision. Initial technical assessments have been carried out and draft plans developed for extensive refurbishment works to the building.  Consideration of these plans along with other options are ongoing and accordingly, no final decision has been taken regarding the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the likelihood of Northern Ireland achieving the 80 per cent renewable energy consumption target by 2030.
answer_text: One of my four critical objectives for the Economy is to reduce carbon emissions; generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change. We must build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to facilitate delivery of the 80% by 2030 target. Whilst my Department has legislative responsibility for the target, collaboration across departments and with wider stakeholders, including industry, will be essential to the successful delivery of this target.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department’s progress on the development of a policy framework for long-duration energy storage.
answer_text: One of my priorities is to reduce carbon emissions and generating sufficient renewable electricity is key to facilitate this change. It is important to build on our previous success in renewables development and the Executive needs to take a collaborative, whole system approach to deliver the 80% by 2030 target.  I recognise the important role that long duration energy storage will play in decarbonising the electricity grid, in particular its ability to utilise excess renewable electricity and export power back to the grid at times of high demand. The Department has commissioned research on interconnection and energy storage here. This research will include analysis on the effect of energy storage on the cost to local consumers, security of supply and decarbonisation, and help inform policy development on both short and long duration energy storage. The Department aims to complete policy development in 2026.
