question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the budget necessary to meet the assessed needs of football clubs via the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: My officials carried out a cost analysis in 2022/2023 which suggests around £120million will be required to modernise performance clubs to a similar standard as that enjoyed in neighbouring jurisdictions.Regarding Grassroots facilities, the ‘DCMS Grassroots Needs Assessment, Full Scoping Report’ published by the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport in June 2022 shows a Northern Ireland deficit on pitch provision at grassroots level which, was at the time of publication, expected to cost in the region of £60million to address. Further work to assess needs at grassroots level is on-going.It should be noted that these are cost estimates and do not assume that funding can be made available at this level from any specific source, including central government. This is simply an estimation of overall potential need calculated at a specific point in time.I expect funding to come from a variety of sources, including match funding sources secured by footballing bodies and clubs themselves. I intend to seek my Executive colleagues’ agreement to allocate additional capital funding to this important Programme. I will also make a strong case for the UK to make a contribution to overall costs to modernise football facilities. I am engaging with the UK Sports Minister in early March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the (i) delivery; and (ii) impact of the Grassroots Facilities Investment Fund 2023-25 programme funded by the UK Government and delivered by the Irish Football Association, as part of the legacy of UK and Ireland UEFA EURO 2028.
answer_text: The delivery of the Grassroots Facilities Investment Fund was not delivered through my Department. The Irish FA or UK Government may be able to provide you with more information on this and the impact of this fund as part of the legacy of the UK and Ireland UEFA Euro 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial assistance his Department has made available to football clubs impacted by flooding.
answer_text: At this time, neither my Department, nor Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, have any funding opportunities available to help football or other sports clubs who have been impacted by flooding.Officials from my Department and Sport NI have met with the sports sector to hear at first hand the impact of recent flooding on sports clubs and facilities.  I am also aware that Sport NI issued a survey to the sports sector to assess the impact of recent flooding on facilities. Sport NI intend to use the information collated to provide advice and signposting for the sports sector as part of a wider sustainability campaign.I have asked that officials from my Department and Sport NI continue to engage with the sector to explore what further support and advice can be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timescale for the adoption of the Zero Emission Vehicle mandate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate was laid by the British Government, Scottish Government, and Welsh Senedd as the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order (VETS) in England, Scotland, and Wales in October 2023 and came into force in those jurisdictions from 03 January 2024. Unfortunately, due to the absence of the Assembly, we were not able to join at that time. I am working with my officials to consider the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to set out the next steps necessary for the allocation of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this commitment has not yet been delivered. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale for the completion of the preliminary design for the (i) A49 Old Ballynahinch Road; and (ii) B6 Saintfield Road.
answer_text: My officials are considering a range of possible measures to improve road safety at these junctions and have recently completed traffic surveys to inform the design process.  Further work to review the outcome of the traffic surveys will need to be undertaken before the preliminary design stage can be completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what immediate steps he intends to take to reform Invest NI.
answer_text: Invest NI has a critical role to play in delivering my Economic Mission.  As the Independent Review of Invest NI made clear, the organisation must restructure and refocus if it is to be effective in the time ahead.	I have engaged with Invest NI’s new CEO to discuss my objectives and the agency’s role in achieving them. Key elements of this reform will include greater conditionality and incentives; a regional structure dedicated to indigenous SMEs and start-ups; and development of industries as well as individual firms. 	In tandem, my officials will continue to implement the Action Plan, co-designed in response to the Review, to enable it to deliver my priorities of improved regional balance and productivity performance, high quality employment and decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will seek to reconfigure the junction of the A26 Glenavy Road and Station Road, Moira, to include a right-turn ghost island.
answer_text: My Department has carried out a feasibility study to consider possible improvements to the Glenavy Road / Station Road / Magheramesk Lane junction, including a right turn lane into Station Road.Initial findings from this study have identified a number of design challenges, particularly relating to relative proximity of the two side roads.  Officials are continuing to seek to develop improvement proposals for this junction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to undertake a reform of Sport NI.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale for the remaining A1 Junction upgrades between Sprucefield and Hillsborough.
answer_text: The Department has a long-term proposal for this section of the A1, which is known as the ‘M1/A1 Link at Sprucefield’ (or Sprucefield Bypass) scheme, and includes the junction at Hillsborough Roundabout.In the interim period the Department is considering measures to improve safety along the central median of this section of the A1. Details on the current position for both the long term and interim schemes can be found in the DfI annual report to Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council 2023, published on 21 November 2023. Please see pages 48 and 50 of the report at the following link:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-lisburn-and-castlereagh-city-council-2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale for the completion of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 between Hillsborough and Loughbrickland.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times increase reliability. and improve road safety.I remain fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory Orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the project is continuing through the development of contract documents and Business Case. In recognition of current and future potential funding challenges, my Department is exploring options to deliver the project in smaller phased packages.  Details of the current status and timescales for of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a1-junctions-phase-2-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale for the completion of the Knockmore Line feasibility study.
answer_text: Translink has received funding through the Union Connectivity Review to undertake a feasibility study for the re-opening of the line between Lisburn and Antrim (Knockmore Line) and the study has now commenced. It will consider both the potential passenger demand and operational model for the line.I can confirm that the study is due to be completed by the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being undertaken to address the back log for MOT tests.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition will launch on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/change-cancel-or-view-your-mot-test-appointment-online.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the increased volume of active travel to Moira Train Station and the associated Park and Ride, whether he will give consideration to an exceptional street-lighting programme for Station Road, Moira.
answer_text: I am aware that people are concerned about street lighting close to Moira train station and I also recognise that parking difficulties in the area are contributing to problems for pedestrians; my officials have been working with Translink to seek to address this problem. A section of footway from the Motorway Bridge to Moira Station was previously widened to accommodate commuters and pedestrians wishing to go to the station and this was recently extended by approximately 110m from the motorway bridge towards Moira. I am also aware that the expansion of Park & Ride facilities at Moira remains a priority project for Translink and completion of this project would reduce use of the overflow car park and consequently footway parking.Street lighting is currently situated at either side of the train station, in the direction of Glenavy as far as the Chestnut Hill Road junction and in the direction of Moira as far as the bridge near Ballycanal Holiday homes. Locations beyond these, which include Station Road, are rural in nature and do not meet the current minimum criteria for the provision of street lighting. Our street lighting policy helps ensure lighting is provided where it is most needed as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts. To avoid setting a precedent it is important that this policy is applied consistently and fairly and as such I am not prepared to approve an exceptional case for Station Road, Moira.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans for the curriculum of the undergraduate social work course to introduce parental alienation training.
answer_text: The NI Degree in Social Work provides robust academic and in-practice training which is designed to equip newly qualified social workers with the skills and knowledge they will need to work in any social work setting. In addition to a range of academic inputs, all student social workers in NI are required to complete a practice placement with children and families, which assists them in dealing with the challenges many families face, including family breakdown and parental alienation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether, considering the scale of the Foyle Cup and its impact on tourism in the region, his Department can ensure that the 2022/23 sponsorship scheme from Tourism NI will fund the event at, or above, the 2019 level to enable the organisers to budget and plan for next year’s event.
answer_text: Tourism NI recognises the positive tourism and broader economic contribution that the Foyle Cup brings to Northern Ireland.In 2019, Tourism NI awarded £30,000 of sponsorship funding to Derry & District Youth Football Association under its National Tourism Events Sponsorship Scheme (NTESS) to stage the Foyle Cup. The award of £30,000 was the highest amount available to a single event under the parameters of the NTESS that year.It is not possible to commit to, or ensure, a specific level of funding to the organisers of the Foyle Cup in 2023. The NTESS operates annually through a competitive, open call process. Each year, each application is considered on its merits and how the event contributes towards the objectives of the scheme. Funding awarded is dependent upon the strength of the proposal, the demand for funding and the budget available in that year. The 2023/24 scheme is due to open in late November 2022. Applications will be assessed against the published criteria, with award outcomes expected to be known by late February 2023. I do hope that, despite the event organiser’s disappointment at their award in 2022/23, they will apply again when the scheme opens later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant AQW 3467/22-27, whether he will give consideration to the reclassification of the Station Road from its rural road status.
answer_text: I can advise that Station Road, Moira is rural in nature with very little frontage development and as such is subject to the national speed. There are no plans to change the current layout or classify the road as urban.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will (i) redevelop the junction of the Station Road and A26 Glenavy Road to improve vehicular access and safety; and (ii) upgrade the footpaths and streetlighting from Moira village to the Station, as part of the new Moira Railway Park and Ride development.
answer_text: My Department has developed a range of preliminary options to improve the Glenavy Road / Station Road / Magheramesk Lane junction, which range in their scope of works, design standards and cost.  My Department will continue to develop these proposals, while taking account of the outcome of the current planning application for the new park and ride facility at Moira Station for which my Department is also a statutory consultee.The planning application for a new park and ride facility involves constructing 489 additional parking spaces north of Moira Station and a new footway connection to the Belfast-bound platform. This is expected to reduce the number of drivers who presently park on Station Road and then walk to Moira Station.I have asked my officials to assess the feasibility of extending the Station Road footway to Moira Station, taking into consideration future changes in pedestrian movements as a result of the new park and ride facility. As Station Road is rural in nature it does not meet the requirements for street lighting and therefore I have no plans to install street lighting on this section of road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to develop a Park and Ride facility in Dromore, Co. Down.
answer_text: I am advised there are currently no plans to develop a Park and Ride facility in Dromore, Co. Down.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department plans to introduce free public transport for everyone under the age of 24; and (ii) for an estimate of the cost of such provision.
answer_text: I fully recognise the challenges facing our citizens in the current cost of living crisis. You will be aware that the fares on our public transport network have been frozen at 2019 prices at this time to ensure that our citizens who rely on public transport will not face additional pressures on their cost of travel. Translink also continues to offer various concessionary and discounted fares including the yLink card for young people aged 16 – 23, which offers one third off bus and rail fares. The 24+ Student Railcard is also available to those in full time education which offers up to one third off rail fares for those that are over 24 years old. The Department will continue to explore what more could be done to help with the cost of public transport during this time. I am mindful that any decision to extend free travel would have to take account of the financial and equality implications and be viewed in the context, of no functioning Executive, an agreed multi year budget and the significant level of inescapable pressures facing my Department this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timeline for a planning decision on the Knockmore Link Road, Lisburn.
answer_text: The planning applications for the link road remain under consideration by my officials and will be progressed to a decision point as soon as possible. When the planning assessment is completed, a recommendation will be made to me for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to outline what funding was made available from his Department or its non-departmental public bodies for the Foyle Cup in July 2022; and (ii) how much funding was made available in the previous three years.
answer_text: The Foyle Cup, held in July 2022, was awarded £9,000 support through Tourism NI’s National Tourism Events Sponsorship Scheme 2022/23.The Foyle Cup did not take place in 2020/21 or 2021/22 therefore no financial support was made available from Tourism NI.The Foyle Cup held in July 2019 was awarded £30,000 of support through Tourism NI’s National Tourism Events Sponsorship Scheme 2019/20.The varying level of awards over the years is primarily a reflection of the significant level of competition for available funding.I appreciate concerns about the reductions in funding available to events, and the impact this can have. I am very aware of the challenges which have been faced by many event promoters over the past few years.Tourism NI recognises the substantial tourism and wider benefits generated by the Foyle Cup and were therefore keen to support it within their sponsorship scheme for 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the cost of the proposed North-South Glider route.
answer_text: The current estimated delivery cost for Belfast Rapid Transit 2 (BRT2) is £142m to £148m, based on the Interim Outline Business Case costings. The estimate is currently being reviewed as part of the finalisation of the Outline Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what preparation she has carried out to deliver 30 hours free childcare per week; and (ii) when this policy will be introduced.
answer_text: I made a Ministerial Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 announcing an expanded and standardised offer for every child in their pre-school year of a minimum of 22.5 hours pre-school education per week, as a core component of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.   This is a vital first step to ensuring equality of provision for all children aged 3-4.  Further proposals, including for other age groups and additional hours, will be considered as part of the ongoing development work on the wider Strategy.    A Review of Childcare Services in NI is already underway and will provide essential evidence to inform this work.I confirmed my intention to bring costed options for Executive consideration in March 2023.    Ultimately, the publication of a draft Strategy for consultation will be subject to Executive consideration and approval, including a commitment to additional and sustained funding for childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline which new sporting events his Department or its non-departmental public bodies has been endeavouring to bring to Northern Ireland since devolution was restored in 2020.
answer_text: The staging of major sporting events has played a crucial part in growth of the tourism sector in Northern Ireland over the past decade, including hosting the Giro d’Italia in 2014, the Irish Open (2012, 2015, 2017 and 2020), Women’s Rugby World Cup 2017, UEFA Women’s U19’s Football Tournament 2017 and one of the most successful Open Championship’s in 2019. These events have not only delivered substantial economic benefits but have also assisted in generating significant social benefits, including high levels of civic pride. 	Officials at Tourism NI and my Department have successfully confirmed the return of The Open to Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025. The decision to bring the Open back to Northern Ireland, is another ringing endorsement for golf and sporting events and came at an important time in relation to Covid recovery for Northern Ireland. 	Additionally, Galgorm and Masserene Golf Clubs hosted the ISPS Handa World Invitational in 2021 and 2022. This innovative event encompasses male and female golfers competing on the same courses at the same time for equal prize money. This unique tournament is at the forefront of innovation in golf and sport in general, pioneering equality. The event, which is promoted by DP World Tour, is also the first of its kind in Northern Ireland given its tri-sanctioned status with the DP World Tour, Ladies European Tour and the LPGA.		Furthermore, officials at Tourism NI and my Department are currently observing the process for the UEFA European Championship five nations bid (UK & Ireland). A number of scoping exercises are also also under way to consider the potential of securing further major sporting events in the next decade, however due to confidentiality reasons I cannot provide details on these at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the cost of living crisis, to provide a timescale for the signing of the contract between her Department and FairShare to allow the purchase of food for delivery to the charity sector.
answer_text: My Department provides grant funding to FareShare. My officials continue to work on options to provide additional support to mitigate the effects of the Cost of Living crisis should further funding become available. 	I remain absolutely committed to finding ways to provide as much support as possible in the coming months but the challenges we face are being made greater due to the continued absences of a functioning Executive and an agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the timescale for the provision in Northern Ireland of a GCSE in British Sign Language.
answer_text: The Department for Education in England is working closely with subject experts, stakeholders and Ofqual, the independent qualifications regulator (England), to develop the draft subject content for a British Sign Language (BSL) GCSE. They will be consulting on this draft and then, once it is agreed, interested awarding bodies will be required to develop specifications. Once this work is completed and a qualification becomes available it can then be accredited for use in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans she has to provide free school meals to all children in reception classes.
answer_text: The Department is currently undertaking a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. Children attending reception classes are included within this work.My Department continues to work to develop a costed options paper and it is intended that a public consultation exercise will take place later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure concerning the implementation of a permit parking scheme, temporary or otherwise, for residents near large sporting venues that experience road closures on match days.
answer_text: There has been no discussion between my Department and the Department for Infrastructure regarding the implementation of a permit-parking scheme.Health and Safety arrangements, including road closures and resident parking permits, are the responsibility of the venue operator in conjunction with the respective local authority and statutory bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is considering re-introducing MOT exemption certificates.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect.  This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The circumstances that provided for the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) to issue Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) have significantly changed, along with the return of normal testing services.  As the DVA is now providing testing capacity at pre-Covid levels it is important, from a road safety perspective, that all vehicles are tested at the earliest opportunity.  As such, there are no plans to issue further TECs at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken to deal with the MOT backlog.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect. This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The next tranche of new vehicle examiners are due to start their training later this month.  To further increase vehicle testing capacity across its network, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  As a first step, the DVA is currently constructing a new test centre at Hydebank in Belfast, which is due to open in 2023.  A second new test centre at Mallusk, which is scheduled to open in 2024, is currently at the planning stage.  These two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help meet the future demand for this service.The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people have successfully been granted assistance through Discretionary Support, over the last three financial years; and (ii) the total amount awarded to applicants, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of applications that have (i) successfully been granted assistance through Discretionary Support, over the last three financial years: and (ii) the total amount awarded to applicants is shown in the table below.Discretionary Support management information does not capture this information broken down by constituency area.  Successful Applications**Total Amount Awarded2019-2051,820£12.8m2020-2177,314£19.496m2021-22*77,622£28.131m*The management information included in this response while accurate at the time it was provided may change when subjected to final reconciliation/verification checks prior to publication as applicable.**Data is not collected on number of individual applicants but rather on number of applications, one individual may make several applications and receive multiple awards. Data collected is the total number of applications resulting in an award made and covers all Discretionary Support provisions including Universal Credit Contingency Fund and Self-Isolation grants as well as standard Discretionary Support grants and loans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what tailored catch-up support is available to deaf and hard-of-hearing children, following the disruption to education caused by the pandemic.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that schools are able to access support for deaf children and young people via the EA Sensory Service, where their needs will be assessed in relation to lost learning or access to the curriculum, with appropriate support provided, where required.  Alongside existing EA pupil support services, the Department has supported the education sector directly since September 2020, through the Engage Programme, to limit any long-term impact of the disruption caused by the pandemic. This programme of additional funding is to support pupils in relation to their learning and engagement, along with their social, emotional and mental health and wellbeing. Engage provides funding for settings, including all special schools, so that they can provide tailored support for individual pupils and small groups of pupils that they assess as being most in need of additional support following the pandemic.Feedback from settings on the Engage programme’s achievements during its first two years continues to be very positive and I decided that its work in supporting those most in need should continue for a third academic year.  I issued a Direction to officials to fund the Engage III programme from September 2022 until the end of December 2022 with a budget of £10m in the first instance.  I intend that the programme will continue during the remainder of the 2022/23 academic year subject to further funding being available.
